extra.message: User input from request.args.get('name') is passed to render_template() and rendered in unsafe_params.html with the '| safe' Jinja2 filter (line 6 of that template), which disables auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the 'name' GET parameter, resulting in reflected XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.cookies.get('name') and request.form.get('name') is passed to render_template() and rendered in unsafe_cookies.html with the '| safe' Jinja2 filter (line 6 of that template), which disables auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via cookie or form data, resulting in XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The MessagesView.post() method accepts user-supplied 'message' and 'from_user' fields from JSON input without authentication and stores them in the database. These values are later rendered in admin/message.html using the '| safe' Jinja2 filter (lines 29-30), which disables auto-escaping. This is a stored XSS vulnerability — any unauthenticated user can inject malicious scripts that execute when an admin views messages.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The username is rendered in the base template with '{{ g.user.username | safe }}', disabling Jinja2 auto-escaping. If a user registers with a malicious username containing JavaScript, it will execute in the browser of any page that uses this base template. This is a stored XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The 'next' parameter from request.args is used directly in redirect() without any validation or sanitization. An attacker can craft a URL with a malicious 'next' parameter (e.g., ?next=https://evil.com) to redirect users to an arbitrary external site.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: The Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'Keep it secret, keep it safe'. This key is used for signing session cookies and other security-sensitive operations. A known secret key allows attackers to forge session cookies and potentially impersonate any user, including admin.

Flask DEBUG mode is set to True in the application configuration. Debug mode enables the interactive debugger which allows arbitrary code execution, and provides detailed error pages that leak sensitive application internals.

User passwords are stored in plaintext in the database (db.Column(db.String(200))). The login view at login/views.py:17 confirms plaintext comparison with 'user.password == password'. Passwords should be hashed using a strong algorithm like bcrypt before storage.

cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

extra.message: Password is compared using direct string equality ('user.password == password') instead of a constant-time comparison function and without any hashing. This confirms plaintext password storage and is also vulnerable to timing attacks.

The User.serialize property includes the plaintext password in its output (line 27: 'password': self.password). This serialized data is returned via the API in UsersView.index() and UsersView.get(), exposing user passwords to anyone with API access.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The MessagesView.post() method lacks any authentication decorator (@admin_required or @login_required). All other write methods in this class require @admin_required. This allows unauthenticated users to create arbitrary messages in the database, which combined with the stored XSS in admin/message.html, enables unauthenticated attackers to execute scripts in the admin's browser.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The after_request handler explicitly sets the X-XSS-Protection header to 0, disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter. While modern browsers have deprecated this header, disabling it removes a defense-in-depth layer for older browsers, especially problematic given the multiple XSS vulnerabilities in this application.

The add_admin management command uses a default password of 'admin' (line 14: password='admin'). If the admin user is created without specifying a custom password, it will have the trivially guessable password 'admin'. The login page template also reveals these credentials: 'Use credentials admin/admin to log in as admin'.

The change_password endpoint accepts POST requests to change a user's password but has no CSRF token validation. The form templates also do not include CSRF tokens. An attacker could trick an authenticated user into submitting a password change request via a crafted page. The same issue affects change_email and send_feedback endpoints.
